article_title: Buy Now, Pay Later Faces Its Regulatory Reckoning as UK Finalizes Rules and New York Proposes Nation's First Licensing Regime

content_preview.title: Buy Now, Pay Later Faces Its Regulatory Reckoning as UK Finalizes Rules and New York Proposes Nation's First Licensing Regime
content_preview.summary: The UK's FCA will enforce full oversight of BNPL products from July 15, while New York has proposed the first comprehensive state licensing framework — filling a gap left by the CFPB's withdrawal of federal consumer protections.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The buy now, pay later industry is entering its most consequential regulatory period since the first BNPL products appeared more than a decade ago. On February 11, the UK's Financial Conduct Authority published [final rules](https://www.reuters.com/business/finance/uk-fca-finalises-buy-now-pay-later-rules-2026-02-11) that will bring the sector under full regulatory oversight from July 15, 2026 — classifying BNPL as "Deferred Payment Credit" subject to creditworthiness checks, disclo...

concerns: Section 75 liability claim and specific NY fee caps ($8 late fee, 16% interest rate) are not confirmed by the press release pages — these details may come from the full regulatory documents (FCA Policy Statement 26/1 and DFS proposed rule text) rather than the cited URLs
Reuters source returns HTTP 401 (paywall), not independently verifiable via automated fetch
recommendations: Consider adding fca.org.uk and richmondfed.org to source allowlist as authoritative government/regulatory sources
Future submissions covering detailed regulatory provisions should cite the specific regulatory document (e.g., PS26/1) rather than only press releases
editor_notes.round_2_notes: Re-review after REQUEST_CHANGES. The dead Financial Times URL (fabricated slug format, HTTP 404) has been replaced with the FCA's own press release, which is live and confirms key claims (July 15 effective date, Deferred Payment Credit classification, affordability checks, May 15 temporary permissions). The irrelevant Bloomberg/Upvest source has been removed. Both requested changes have been addressed satisfactorily. FCA and NY Governor sources independently verified via WebFetch.
